coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based luminescent materials with aggregation-induced emission and solvent crystallization-induced emission behaviors
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Jianliang
Qin, Fu
He, Pinyi
Wu, Shuang
Zhu, Yaqing
Yuan, Guojun
Wang, Xinyu
Yu, Xu
Ren, Lili
coredata.dc:description: As a novel carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterial, the optical properties of carbon dots (CDs) in dispersed state have been extensively studied. Nevertheless, carbon dots-based fluorescent materials with high luminescence efficiency in both aggregated and dispersed states are rarely reported. Herein, hydrothermal treatment of p-phenylenediamine (PPD), followed by column chromatography, the obtained R-CDs showing excitation-independent red fluorescence (FL) emission. Significantly, yellow fluorescent powder (Y-CDs) with strong solid-state emission has been prepared by the reaction of R-CDs and salicylaldehyde (SA). More interestingly, the synthesized Y-CDs showed obvious aggregation-induced and solvent crystallization-induced FL enhancement behaviors. These findings have a certain reference significance for the development of CDs-based materials with high luminescence efficiency in both aggregated and dispersed states.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aggregation-induced emission
Solvent crystallization-induced emission
Solid-state fluorescence
Surface states

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra for R-CDs solution. (b) The fluorescence emission spectra of the R-CDs dispersed in ethanol at different excitation-wavelength.
(a) TEM image of R-CDs. (b) The corresponding particle size histogram of R-CDs. (c) Raman spectrum of the R-CDs. (d) FT-IR spectrum of R-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra for Y-CDs powders. (b) The fluorescence images of Y-CDs in aggregated state (left) and DMSO solutions (right) under UV lamp. (c) The fluorescence images of Y-CDs in aggregated state (left) and DMSO crystals (right) under UV lamp.
Schematic illustration the synthesis of R-CDs.
Schematic illustration the synthesis of Y-CDs.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based luminescent materials with aggregation-induced emission and solvent crystallization-induced emission behaviors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment and Chemical Engineering, Anhui Vocational and Technical College, Hefei 230011, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environment and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment and Chemical Engineering, Anhui Vocational and Technical College, Hefei 230011, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a novel carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterial, the optical properties of carbon dots (CDs) in dispersed state have been extensively studied. Nevertheless, carbon dots-based fluorescent materials with high luminescence efficiency in both aggregated and dispersed states are rarely reported. Herein, hydrothermal treatment of p-phenylenediamine (PPD), followed by column chromatography, the obtained R-CDs showing excitation-independent red fluorescence (FL) emission. Significantly, yellow fluorescent powder (Y-CDs) with strong solid-state emission has been prepared by the reaction of R-CDs and salicylaldehyde (SA). More interestingly, the synthesized Y-CDs showed obvious aggregation-induced and solvent crystallization-induced FL enhancement behaviors. These findings have a certain reference significance for the development of CDs-based materials with high luminescence efficiency in both aggregated and dispersed states.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), which are an emerging and highly-promising type of carbon-based fluorescent material, are attracting broad research attention because of convenient synthesis, low toxicity, bio-compatibility and excellent optical and electrical properties [1–11]. These advantages make CDs have great application potential in many aspects, including light emitting diodes (LEDs) [6,12–15], sonodynamic and photodynamic therapy [16–17], anti-counterfeiting [18–20], bioimaging [2,10,21], photocatalysis [22], electrocatalysis [23], energy storage [24], ionic detectors [25] and solar cells [26]. However, like organic fluorophore, most CDs often suffer from aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ) effects, which hinders the development and application of CDs [13,27]. To solve the ACQ effects in CDs, a routine way is to introduce dispersion matrices that can ensure sufficient distance between the CDs’ particles. Unfortunately, the matrices increase the cost of preparation and affect the optical performance of CDs. Worse, when more CDs are introduced into the matrices, FL quenching still occurs [28].

Since Tang et al. reported solid-state FL of 1-methyl-1,2,3,4,5-pentaphenylsilole, a large number of fluorescent materials with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) have been developed by researchers all over the world [29–31]. So far, CDs-based fluorescent materials with AIE optical characteristics prepared by different strategies also have been reported successively [28,32–37]. These quenching-resistant CDs powders show red-shifted emission with obvious color changes compared to solution-state FL, showing remarkable dual-state emission (DSE) [27,31–32,38]. DSE in both solution and solid state is very important for understanding the mechanisms and properties of fluorescent molecules and their practical applications. However, it is a great challenge to avoid the obvious red-shift FL behaviors in aggregated state of CDs with DSE characteristics.

Motivated by the aforementioned difficulties, in this work we synthesized R-CDs with excitation-independent red FL via facile hydrothermal treatment of p-phenylenediamine (PPD). And then for surface modification, salicylaldehyde (SA) is connected to R-CDs and resulting obtained CDs-based powders (Y-CDs) possessed yellow FL. The Y-CDs show obvious aggregation-induced emission and solvent crystallization-induced emission optical behaviors. Surprisingly, in both cases, the DSE of the Y-CDs show no significant color change. Based on the experimental results, we propose a solvent crystallization strategy to achieve “dual-mode FL emission” of CDs-based AIE materials.

In order to understand the structure of R-CDs generated by column chromatography, we also took TEM image, Raman spectrum and FT-IR spectrum. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, R-CDs are uniformly diffused without aggregation, with an average particle size of 1.85 nm (Fig. 2b). The Raman spectrum of R-CDs show two peaks at 1381 and 1527 cm−1 (Fig. 2c), referring to disordered (D band) and graphite (G band) carbon structures, indicating that sp3 and sp2 hybrid carbon networks in R-CDs [12]. The FT-IR spectrum of R-CDs is shown in Fig. 2d. The peak at 3450 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H, the peak at 2923 cm−1 is contributed from C-H. The stretching vibration of the aromatic ring skeleton (C = C/C = N) is responsible for absorption peak at 1656 cm−1. The C = O groups could be responsible for the peak at 1631 cm−1. The stretching vibrations of C-N and C-O have absorption peaks located 1384 and 1116 cm−1, respectively [40–42]. The above results suggest that the R-CDs may be partial graphitized carbon particles containing functional groups such as carbonyl, amine and hydroxyl [43–44].

Furthermore, the solvent crystallization-induced emission characteristics of Y-CDs in dispersed state are studied. As presented in Fig. 3c (right), when the DMSO solutions of Y-CDs are at low temperature, the system becomes crystallized and exhibits stronger emission than Y-CDs powders (Fig. 3c, left) with “naked eye”. This interesting phenomenon is attributed to the fact that solvent crystallization significantly hardens the Y-CDs structures, limiting the molecular movement and reducing the non-radiative transition process, which make Y-CDs effectively emit FL [51]. More importantly, this phenomenon enables CDs-based fluorescent materials to achieve highly efficient emission of dispersed and aggregated states without changing their FL colors [27–32].

In summary, we developed CDs-based AIE fluorescence material (Y-CDs) with non-color-changing efficient luminescence in both aggregated and dispersed states, and proposed its possible chemical structure and fluorescence mechanism. On the one hand, the luminescent Y-CDs shows obvious AIE characteristics; on the other hand, the crystalline solvent can induce strong FL emission of Y-CDs and keep the same luminous color. This work establishes a new strategy for rationally manipulating the dual-state optical characteristics of CDs, paving the way for new CD applications to emerge.

Jianliang Bai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Fu Qin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Pinyi He: Formal analysis, Investigation. Shuang Wu: Formal analysis, Investigation. Yaqing Zhu: Methodology, Investigation. Guojun Yuan: Methodology, Investigation. Xinyu Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Xu Yu: Methodology, Investigation. Lili Ren: Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22275035).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2023.110614.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultralong lifetime and efficient room temperature phosphorescent carbon dots through multiconfinement structure design

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Sun, S. Liu, L. Sun, S. Wu, G. Hu, X. Pang, A. T. Smith, C. Hu, S. Zeng, W. Wang, Y. Liu, M. Zheng, Ultralong lifetime and efficient room temperature phosphorescent carbon dots through multiconfinement structure design, Nat. Commun. 11 (2020) 5591.
